html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 宣室志 - The title of the text, indicating it is a record or collection of stories or anecdotes.
  • 唐故尚书李公诜镇北门时 - During the time when the former Tang Minister Li Gong Shen was stationed at the northern gate.
  • 有道士尹君者 - There was a Daoist named Yin Jun.
  • 隐晋山 - He lived in seclusion in Jin Mountain.
  • 不食粟 - He did not eat grain.
  • 常饵柏叶 - He often ate pine leaves.
  • 虽发尽白 - Even though his hair was completely white.
  • 而容状若童子 - But his appearance was like that of a child.
  • 往往独游城市 - He often wandered alone in the city.
  • 里中有老父年八十余者 - In the village, there was an old man over eighty years old.
  • 顾谓人曰 - He turned to the people and said.
  • 吾孩提时尝见李翁言 - I saw Li Gong when I was a child and he said.
  • 李翁，吾外祖也 - Li Gong was my maternal grandfather.
  • 且曰 - And he said.
  • 我年七岁，已识尹君矣 - When I was seven years old, I had already known Yin Jun.
  • 迨今七十余年 - Until now, over seventy years.
  • 而尹君容状如旧 - But Yin Jun's appearance remained unchanged.
  • 得非神仙乎 - Is he not a deity?
  • 吾且老，自度能几何为人间人 - I am old, and I can only estimate how much longer I can remain in the human world.
  • 汝方壮，当志尹君之容状 - You are still strong, so you should remember Yin Jun's appearance.
  • 自是及今，七十余岁矣 - From then until now, over seventy years have passed.
  • 而尹君曾无老色 - But Yin Jun never showed any signs of aging.
  • 岂非以千百岁为瞬息耶 - Is it not as if a thousand years is just a moment?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐 - The Tang Dynasty (618-907 AD), a period of significant cultural and literary achievement in Chinese history.
  • 尚书 - A high-ranking official in the Tang government, often responsible for administrative and military affairs.
  • 李公诜 - The name of the Tang Minister, a historical figure whose actions and presence set the context for the narrative.
  • 北门 - Refers to the northern gate of the capital city, a strategic and symbolic location.
  • 道士 - A Daoist practitioner, often associated with spiritual and mystical practices.
  • 晋山 - A specific mountain where Yin Jun lived in seclusion, symbolizing a place of retreat and spiritual cultivation.
  • 柏叶 - Pine leaves, often associated with longevity and immortality in Chinese culture.
  • 神仙 - Deities or immortals, figures who have achieved a state of eternal life and supernatural powers.
  • 外祖 - Maternal grandfather, a familial relationship that adds a personal dimension to the narrative.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces the main character and the setting. The phrase '有道士尹君者' is a nominal clause that provides additional information about Yin Jun.
  • This sequence describes Yin Jun's lifestyle and diet. The parallel structure emphasizes his ascetic practices.
  • This contrast highlights Yin Jun's youthful appearance despite his age.
  • This simple sentence describes Yin Jun's behavior in the city.
  • This sentence introduces the old man and his direct address to others.
  • This sequence sets the old man's recollection of a past encounter with Li Gong.
  • This long sequence describes the old man's memory of Yin Jun and his unchanged appearance.
  • This rhetorical question expresses the old man's speculation about Yin Jun's status.
  • This sentence reflects the old man's awareness of his mortality.
  • This advice is directed at the younger generation.
  • This sequence reinforces the passage of time and Yin Jun's timeless appearance.
  • This rhetorical question expresses the old man's wonder at Yin Jun's apparent immortality.

Proper Nouns

  • 唐 - The Tang Dynasty.
  • 李公诜 - The name of the Tang Minister.
  • 尹君 - The name of the Daoist practitioner.
  • 晋山 - The name of the mountain where Yin Jun lived in seclusion.
  • 李翁 - The name of the old man's maternal grandfather.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 従事 - Refers to an official or attendant, often with a connotation of service or duty.
  • 好奇者 - Means 'one who is fond of the unusual' or 'one who seeks the extraordinary.'
  • 旬休 - Refers to a ten-day rest period, a traditional Chinese calendar system.
  • 驅驾 - Means 'to drive or lead' a carriage or vehicle.
  • 馆于公署 - Means 'to lodge in the government office' or 'to be housed in the official residence.'
  • 终日与同席 - Means 'to sit together all day' or 'to share the same seat for the entire day.'
  • 异香 - Refers to a strange or unusual fragrance.
  • 肌中发 - Means 'emitting from the flesh' or 'emanating from the body.'
  • 浮图氏 - Refers to Buddhism, a term derived from the Sanskrit 'Buddha.'
  • 殊致 - Means 'different goals' or 'distinct aims.'
  • 密以堇斟致汤中 - Means 'secretly mixed with a poisonous substance into the soup.'
  • 吾其死乎 - Means 'I am going to die, I suppose.'
  • 貌衰齿堕 - Means 'appearance declined and teeth fell out.'
  • 治其丧 - Means 'to handle the funeral arrangements.'
  • 葬尹君于汾水西二十里 - Means 'to bury Mr. Yin twenty li west of the Fen River.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 冯翊严公绶 - Refers to Yan Gong Shou, a historical figure known for his interest in the unusual.
  • 尹之得道 - Refers to Yin's attainment of the Dao, suggesting a spiritual or mystical achievement.
  • 北门帅 - Refers to the commander of the northern gate, a position of military authority.
  • 佛氏与黄老固殊致 - A comparison between Buddhism (佛氏) and Daoism (黄老), highlighting their different philosophical goals.
  • 汾水 - Refers to the Fen River, a major river in Shanxi Province, China.

Proper Nouns

  • 冯翊严公绶 - Yan Gong Shou, a historical figure.
  • 尹 - Yin, a Daoist who attained the Dao.
  • 汾水 - The Fen River, a major river in Shanxi Province, China.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 明年秋 - Temporal phrase meaning 'the following autumn,' indicating the time of an event.
  • 示之以死 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'showed them death,' suggesting a demonstration of invulnerability or a supernatural event.
  • 尸解 - Daoist concept of immortality where the physical body is left behind, often described as a 'dissolution of the corpse.'
  • 绝粒 - Refers to fasting or abstaining from food, often associated with spiritual practices.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 尹君 - Proper name likely referring to a Daoist figure or practitioner.
  • 朱太虚 - Proper name, likely a Daoist or scholar.
  • 严公 - Title referring to a high-ranking official, likely a governor or provincial leader.
  • 照圣观 - Daoist temple or place of worship.
  • 晋山 - Specific mountain, likely a place of significance in the narrative.
  • 北门 - Reference to a location, possibly a city gate or a symbolic place.
  • 堇斟 - Poisonous drink or substance, likely a metaphor for a dangerous or lethal substance.
  • 仙人不死 - Daoist belief in the possibility of achieving immortality.

Proper Nouns

  • 尹君 - Mr. Yin
  • 朱太虚 - Zhu Taixu
  • 严公 - Governor Yan
  • 照圣观 - Zhaosheng Temple
  • 晋山 - Jin Mountain
  • 北门 - Northern Gate
  • 堇斟 - Jin Zhen

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 尝一日 - Means 'once upon a time' or 'one day,' introducing a narrative.
  • 神骨甚孤秀 - Describes a person's appearance as solitary and exceptionally refined.
  • 邀游仙都中 - Means 'to wander in the fairy realm,' referring to a spiritual or mythical journey.
  • 尘俗之人 - Refers to an ordinary, worldly person.
  • 仙都甚近 - Means 'the fairy realm is very close,' suggesting that the divine is accessible.
  • 力去也 - Means 'to make an effort to go there,' emphasizing the need for action.
  • 导其径 - Means 'to guide the path,' referring to leading someone to a destination.
  • 备食于商山逆旅中 - Means 'to prepare food at an inn in Shangshan,' indicating preparation for a journey.
  • 遇捀即犒于商山而馈焉 - Means 'to reward and feed the traveler in Shangshan when they meet,' describing a ritual of hospitality.
  • 所诣者 - Means 'the place one is going to,' referring to the destination.
  • 原游稚川 - Means 'originally intended to visit Zhi Chuan,' referring to a specific place or realm.
  • 捀子导师而去矣 - Means 'the traveler will guide them away,' indicating a supernatural or divine intervention.
  • 喜且甚 - Means 'very happy,' expressing strong emotion.
  • 禄山败 - Refers to the defeat of An Lushan, a significant historical event.
  • 上自蜀门还长安 - Means 'the emperor returned from Shu to Chang'an,' indicating a return to the capital.
  • 天下无事 - Means 'the world is at peace,' describing a period of stability.
  • 意稍怠 - Means 'the intention became a little lazy,' indicating a loss of enthusiasm.
  • 见欺 - Means 'to be deceived,' suggesting doubt or mistrust.
  • 治装 - Means 'to prepare for travel,' indicating readiness for departure.
  • 诚能游稚川，死不悔 - Means 'if one can truly travel to Zhi Chuan, they will not regret it even if it means death,' expressing a strong commitment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道士乔君 - Refers to a Daoist priest named Qiao Jun, a figure in Daoist tradition.
  • 契虚 - A character in the narrative, likely a practitioner or seeker of Daoist teachings.
  • 仙都 - Refers to the fairy realm, a common theme in Daoist and Chinese mythology.
  • 商山 - A mountain in Shaanxi province, historically associated with Daoist hermits and scholars.
  • 逆旅 - Means 'inn' or 'rest stop,' a place for travelers to stay.
  • 稚川 - Likely refers to a specific place or realm in Daoist mythology, possibly associated with the legendary figure Ge Hong.
  • 禄山 - An Lushan, a rebel leader during the Tang Dynasty, whose defeat marked a turning point in Chinese history.
  • 上自蜀门还长安 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang returning to Chang'an after fleeing to Shu during the An Lushan Rebellion.
  • 天下无事 - Describes a period of peace and stability in Chinese history.

Proper Nouns

  • 道士乔君 - Daoist priest Qiao Jun.
  • 契虚 - A character in the narrative.
  • 仙都 - The fairy realm.
  • 商山 - A mountain in Shaanxi province.
  • 逆旅 - Inn or rest stop.
  • 稚川 - A specific place or realm in Daoist mythology.
  • 禄山 - An Lushan, a historical figure.
  • 上自蜀门还长安 - Emperor Xuanzong of Tang returning to Chang'an.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 俱至 - Both arrived, indicating that both individuals reached a specific location together.
  • 治具 - To prepare equipment or set up supplies.
  • 即登 - Immediately ascended, emphasizing the immediacy of their action.
  • 涉危险，逾岩巘 - Crossed dangerous terrain and climbed over rocks and peaks, describing their journey through challenging landscapes.
  • 三日，洞水方绝 - Three days, finally stopped, indicating the duration it took for the water to cease flowing.
  • 昏晦不可辨 - Dark and dim, unrecognizable, describing the difficult conditions inside the cave.
  • 真神仙都也 - A true land of immortals, a metaphorical description of the beautiful landscape they encountered.
  • 仙都且近，何为彷徨耶 - The land of immortals is already near, why hesitate? A rhetorical question encouraging continued progress.
  • 其上坦平 - Flat and level, describing the top of the mountain.
  • 下视川原 - Looking down, rivers and plains, indicating the expansive view from the mountain.
  • 见积水无穷 - Saw endless accumulation of water, describing the vast body of water they encountered.
  • 水中有石径，横尺余，纵且百里余 - A stone path in the water, about one foot wide, over a hundred miles long, describing the unique path.
  • 捀子引契虚蹑石迳而去 - Led, stepped on, and went away, describing their journey along the stone path.
  • 前有巨木，烟影繁茂 - In front of them was a large tree with dense shadows, describing the tree they encountered.
  • 捀子登木长啸久之 - Climbed the tree, sang loudly for a long time, describing the action of the character.
  • 巨绳系一行橐 - A large rope tied a row of bags, describing the setup they encountered.
  • 命契虚暝目坐橐中 - Ordered, closed eyes, sat in the bag, describing the action of the character.
  • 仅半日，捀子曰：“师可寤而视矣。” - Only half a day, the master can wake up and see now, indicating the passage of time and the character's instruction.
  • 契虚既望，已在山顶 - After looking around, they were already at the top of the mountain, describing the final location.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 捀子与契虚 - Characters in a story or legend, possibly representing different aspects of a journey or spiritual quest.
  • 蓝田 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location in China known for its natural beauty or historical significance.
  • 玉山 - A mountain name, possibly a mythical or significant mountain in Chinese literature or folklore.
  • 神仙都 - A metaphorical term for a place of immortals or a paradise, often used in Chinese literature to describe idyllic or supernatural landscapes.
  • 仙都 - Another term for a land of immortals, reinforcing the idea of a mystical or divine place.

Proper Nouns

  • 捀子 - A character name.
  • 契虚 - A character name.
  • 蓝田 - A place name.
  • 玉山 - A mountain name.
  • 神仙都 - A metaphorical term for a place of immortals.
  • 仙都 - Another term for a land of immortals.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 見有城邑宫阙 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'seeing cities and palaces,' used to set the scene.
  • 玑玉交映 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'interwoven with pearls and jade,' used to describe the appearance of the palaces.
  • 云物之外 - Phrase meaning 'beyond the clouds,' indicating a transcendent or ethereal setting.
  • 挈而至此 - Phrase meaning 'brought here,' used to describe the action of bringing someone to a place.
  • 呵禁极严 - Phrase meaning 'strictly forbidden and prohibited,' indicating the strictness of the rules.
  • 讯曰 - Phrase meaning 'asked,' used to describe the act of questioning.
  • 真不可留于此 - Phrase meaning 'cannot be left here,' indicating a decision or command.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 稚川 - Refers to a place or realm, possibly a mythical or legendary location.
  • 仙童百辈 - Refers to a hundred or many young immortals, indicating a celestial or divine setting.
  • 真君 - Refers to a true or genuine deity or immortal, often used in Taoist contexts.
  • 三彭之仇 - Refers to an ancient enemy or rival, possibly a reference to the Three Kings of Peng (彭祖, 彭武, 彭越), who are significant figures in Chinese mythology.

Proper Nouns

  • 翠霞亭 - Specific location in the narrative, likely a pavilion or building in a celestial realm.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 其亭亘空 - Describes a pavilion or hall that stretches into the sky, suggesting grandeur or a connection to the heavens.
  • 居槛云矗 - Describes a building or structure that rises like clouds, emphasizing its height and majesty.
  • 見一人袒而瞬目 - Describes a person who is naked and blinking, which could be interpreted as a strange or supernatural appearance.
  • 發長数十尺 - Describes hair that is several feet long, indicating an unusual or supernatural feature.
  • 凝腻黯黑 - Describes the hair as thick, glossy, and dark, suggesting a mystical or otherworldly quality.
  • 洞莹心目 - Describes the hair as shining brightly, capturing the viewer's attention and filling their eyes with light.
  • 捀子谓契虚曰 - A dialogue where '捀子' (a guide or interpreter) speaks to '契虚' (a visitor or disciple).
  • 尔可谒而拜 - An instruction to bow and pay respects.
  • 此人为谁何瞬目乎 - A question asking who the person is and why they are blinking.
  • 此人杨外郎也 - Identifies the person as '杨外郎' (Yang Wailang), a member of the royal family of the Sui dynasty.
  • 属隋末，天下分磔，兵甲大扰 - Describes a period of chaos and division at the end of the Sui dynasty.
  • 因避地居山，今已得道 - Explains that the person retreated to the mountains and has since attained enlightenment.
  • 此非瞬目，乃彻视也 - Clarifies that the blinking is not a physical act but a form of spiritual vision.
  • 夫彻视者，寓目于人世耳 - Explains that spiritual vision allows one to see beyond the physical world.
  • 请寤其目，可乎 - A request to open the person's spiritual vision.
  • 外郎忽寤而四视，其光益著若日月之照 - Describes the moment when the spiritual vision is opened, and the light becomes even more intense, like the sun and moon.
  • 契虚悸背汗，毛发尽劲 - Describes the physical reaction of the visitor, who is filled with awe and fear.
  • 又见一人卧石壁之下 - Describes another person lying under a stone wall.
  • 此人姓乙，支润其名 - Identifies the person as '乙支润' (Yi Zhirun).
  • 亦人间之人，得道而至此 - Explains that this person is also from the human world but has attained enlightenment and arrived here.
  • 已而捀子引契虚归 - Describes the guide leading the visitor back.
  • 其道途皆前时之涉历 - Indicates that the path taken is the same as before.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 隋氏宗室 - Refers to a member of the royal family of the Sui dynasty.
  • 南宫 - Refers to a palace or government office in the Sui dynasty.
  • 得道 - Refers to achieving enlightenment or spiritual awakening.
  • 彻视 - Refers to a form of spiritual vision or clairvoyance.
  • 人间之人 - Refers to a person from the human world, as opposed to a deity or supernatural being.

Proper Nouns

  • 捀子 - A guide or interpreter.
  • 契虚 - A visitor or disciple.
  • 杨外郎 - A member of the royal family of the Sui dynasty.

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 契虛因問捀子曰 - Sets up a dialogue between a master and a student.
  • 吾向者谒见真君 - Refers to paying respects or visiting a revered figure, likely a Taoist god.
  • 三彭之仇 - Refers to the 'three rebels' or 'three enemies' in Taoist cosmology.
  • 三尸之姓 - Refers to the 'surname' or 'name' of the 'three ghosts' or 'three corpses' in Taoist cosmology.
  • 每至庚申日，籍于上帝 - Refers to the Gengshen day, traditionally associated with the reporting of sins to the gods.
  • 故学仙者，当先绝其三尸 - Refers to the necessity of eliminating the 'three ghosts' for those seeking immortality.
  • 契虚悟其事 - Indicates that Che Xu understood the matter.
  • 自是而归 - Indicates that Che Xu returned from then on.
  • 因庐于太白山，绝粒啄气 - Indicates that Che Xu lived in Taibai Mountain, abstaining from food and breathing air.
  • 未尝以稚川之事语于人 - Indicates that Che Xu never spoke about the matter of Zhiyuan to others.
  • 贞元中，徙居华山下 - Indicates that Che Xu moved to live at the foot of Mount Hua during the reign of Emperor Dezong of Tang.
  • 郑君异其不食，而骨状丰秀 - Indicates that Zheng was surprised by Che Xu's fasting and his well-formed and handsome appearance.
  • 契虚乃以稚川之事告于郑 - Indicates that Che Xu told Zheng about the matter of Zhiyuan.
  • 郑好奇者，既闻其事，且欢且惊 - Indicates that Zheng, being curious, was both happy and surprised upon hearing the story.
  • 及自关东回，重至契虚舍，其契虚已遁去，竟不知所在 - Indicates that after returning from the east of the pass, Zheng returned to Che Xu's dwelling, but Che Xu had already fled and no one knew where he was.
  • 郑君常传其事，谓之《稚川记》 - Indicates that Zheng often told the story and called it *The Record of Zhiyuan*.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 三尸 - In Taoist cosmology, the 'three ghosts' or 'three corpses' are believed to reside in the human body and report one's sins to the gods.
  • 庚申日 - In Chinese folklore, the Gengshen day is associated with the reporting of sins to the gods.
  • 学仙者 - Refers to individuals who practice Taoist alchemy and seek immortality.
  • 太白山 - A famous mountain in Shaanxi Province, known for its association with Taoist practices and immortality.
  • 华山 - Another famous mountain in Shaanxi Province, known for its association with Taoist practices and immortality.
  • 稚川 - Likely refers to Ge Hong (葛洪), a famous Taoist alchemist and writer from the Jin Dynasty.
  • 贞元 - A reign title of Emperor Dezong of Tang (779-805 CE).
  • 荥阳 - A place name in China, likely referring to a region where the characters in the story are from.
  • 吴兴 - A place name in China, likely referring to a region where the characters in the story are from.
  • 长安 - The capital of the Tang Dynasty, located in present-day Xi'an, Shaanxi Province.

[9]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 十仙子 - Term meaning 'ten fairy sons' or 'ten celestial beings,' likely referring to a group of divine figures.
  • 御卿云而下 - Classical expression meaning 'riding on clouds and descending,' describing a divine or supernatural arrival.
  • 列于庭 - Means 'arranging themselves in the courtyard,' indicating a formal gathering.
  • 各执乐器而奏之 - Means 'each holding an instrument and playing it,' describing the performance.
  • 其度曲清越 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'their tunes were clear and ethereal,' praising the music's quality.
  • 真仙府之音也 - Means 'truly the music of the celestial realm,' emphasizing the divine nature of the music.
  • 乐阕 - Means 'when the music ended,' marking the conclusion of the performance.
  • 陛下知此乐乎 - Respectful question meaning 'Do you, O Emperor, know this music?'
  • 今愿传授陛下 - Means 'now I wish to teach it to Your Majesty,' indicating an offer of instruction.
  • 为圣唐正始音 - Means 'to be the correct beginning sound for the Holy Tang,' suggesting a foundational role for the music.
  • 与夫《咸池》、《大夏》固不同矣 - Means 'it is fundamentally different from the *Xianchi* and *Daxia*,' contrasting the new music with established classical pieces.
  • 玄宗喜甚 - Means 'Emperor Xuanzong was very pleased,' expressing his reaction.
  • 即传受焉 - Means 'he immediately accepted the transmission,' indicating his decision to learn the music.
  • 俄而寤 - Means 'soon he woke up,' describing the transition from dream to waking.
  • 其余响犹若在听 - Means 'the lingering echoes still seemed to be heard,' describing the aftereffect of the dream.
  • 遽命玉笛吹而习之 - Means 'he hastily ordered a jade flute to be played and practiced it,' indicating his immediate action.
  • 尽得其节奏 - Means 'he fully grasped its rhythm,' describing his success in learning.
  • 嘿不泄 - Means 'kept silent and did not reveal it,' indicating his secrecy.
  • 及晓，听政于紫宸殿 - Means 'when morning came, he listened to government affairs in the Purple宸 Hall,' describing his daily routine.
  • 宰臣姚崇、宋璟入 - Means 'the prime ministers Yao Chong and Song Jing entered,' introducing the key figures.
  • 奏事于御前 - Means 'presented matters before the emperor,' describing their role.
  • 玄宗俛若不闻 - Means 'Emperor Xuanzong seemed not to hear them,' indicating his preoccupation.
  • 二相惧，又奏之 - Means 'the two prime ministers were afraid and presented it again,' describing their repeated attempt.
  • 玄宗即起，卒不顾二相 - Means 'Emperor Xuanzong immediately stood up and finally did not look at the two prime ministers,' indicating his continued preoccupation.
  • 二相益恐，趋出 - Means 'the two prime ministers became even more afraid and hurried out,' describing their reaction.
  • 时高力士侍于玄宗 - Means 'at that time, Gao Lishi was serving Emperor Xuanzong,' introducing the key figure.
  • 即奏曰 - Means 'immediately reported,' indicating his action.
  • 宰相请事，陛下宜面决可否 - Means 'the prime ministers requested matters, and Your Majesty should personally decide whether to approve or not,' suggesting his advice.
  • 向者崇、璟所言，皆军国大政 - Means 'what Yao and Song said earlier were all major state affairs,' describing the content of their report.
  • 而陛下卒不顾，岂二相有罪乎 - Means 'but Your Majesty finally did not look at them, could the two prime ministers be guilty?' indicating his concern.
  • 玄宗笑曰 - Means 'Emperor Xuanzong laughed and said,' indicating his response.
  • 我昨夕梦仙人奏乐曰《紫云曲》 - Means 'I dreamed last night that a fairy played music called *Ziyun Qu*,' describing his dream.
  • 因以授我 - Means 'so he taught it to me,' indicating the source of the music.
  • 我失其节奏 - Means 'I lost its rhythm,' describing his difficulty.
  • 由是嘿而习之 - Means 'so I kept silent and practiced it,' indicating his action.
  • 故不暇听二相奏事 - Means 'so I had no time to listen to the two prime ministers' reports,' explaining his preoccupation.
  • 即于衣中出玉笛，以示力士 - Means 'he immediately took out a jade flute from his clothes and showed it to Gao Lishi,' indicating his action.
  • 是日，力士至中书 - Means 'that day, Gao Lishi went to the Secretariat,' describing his action.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 别久无恙乎 - Have you been well since we last met?; a phrase implying a prior acquaintance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐故剑南节度使太尉兼中书令韦皋 - A high-ranking official of the Tang dynasty; 剑南节度使 refers to the military governor of the Jiannan region (modern Sichuan and parts of Yunnan); 太尉兼中书令 are two of the highest official titles in the Tang bureaucracy, indicating his high rank.
  • 韦皋 - A historical figure, a Tang dynasty general and administrator who served in the region of Sichuan and was known for his military and administrative skills.
  • 胡僧 - A monk from the Western Regions (today's Central Asia); often associated with Buddhism in China during the Tang dynasty.
  • 诸葛武侯 - Zhuge Liang, a revered strategist and chancellor of the state of Shu during the Three Kingdoms period (220–280 CE); often venerated as a symbol of wisdom, loyalty, and virtue.
  • 蜀 - The ancient kingdom of Shu, located in the Sichuan Basin, one of the three kingdoms in the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 剑门 - Jianmen Pass, a famous mountain pass in Sichuan, historically a strategic military location.
  • 金吾节制剑南军 - Refers to the military command structure in the Tang dynasty; 金吾卫 (Jinwu Wei) was a military guard unit, and 节制 (jiezhi) means to command or control.
  • 累迁太尉兼中书令 - Indicates a series of promotions to high-ranking positions.
  • 在蜀十八年 - Spent eighteen years in Shu; referring to the time Wei Gao spent in Sichuan as a military governor.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 因赂九仙公主左右，得以温泉 - This phrase can be read as a cause-and-effect relationship: 'by bribing those close to Princess Jiu Xian, he gained access to hot springs.'
  • 命内臣选硕学僧十辈，与方士议论 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of actions: 'ordered the inner court officials to select ten learned monks, and they engaged in discussions with Daoist scholars.'
  • 演畅玄奥，发挥疑义 - This phrase can be read as two parallel actions: 'elaborate on profound mysteries' and 'clarify ambiguous points.'
  • 上奇其辩，诏赐银印朱绶，拜四门博士 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of events: 'the emperor was amazed by his eloquence, issued an edict granting him a silver seal and red sash, and appointed him as a Four Gates Doctor.'
  • 日侍左右，赐甲第洎金钱缯采以千数 - This phrase can be read as two parallel actions: 'served at the emperor's side daily' and 'bestowed on him a mansion and thousands of gold coins and silk.'
  • 惠达遂自蓟门入长安访之 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of actions: 'Huidada then traveled from Jimo to Chang'an to visit him.'
  • 以为收债于己，甚不怿 - This phrase can be read as a cause-and-effect relationship: 'thinking that Huidada came to collect debts from him, he was very unhappy.'
  • 因告去，既以北归月余 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of actions: 'he then took his leave, and after returning north for more than a month.'
  • 虑其再来，即密书与蓟门帅张廷珪 - This phrase can be read as a cause-and-effect relationship: 'fearing that he would return again, he then secretly wrote a letter to Zhang Tinggui, the commander of Jimo.'
  • 诬毁公缮完兵革，将为逆谋 - This phrase can be read as a serious accusation: 'accusing you of repairing weapons and armor, planning to rebel.'
  • 人亦颇有知者，积毁销金，不可不戒 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of ideas: 'many people also knew about it, repeated slander can erode even gold, so one must be cautious.'
  • 廷珪惊怒，即召惠达鞭杀之 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of actions: 'Zhang Tinggui was shocked and angry, and immediately summoned Huidada to beat him to death.'
  • 后数日，夜光忽见惠达来庭中 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of events: 'a few days later, Yeguang suddenly saw Huidada appear in the courtyard.'
  • 骂夜光曰，我以七十万钱资汝西上，奈何遽相诬谤，使我冤死 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of actions: 'he cursed Yeguang saying, I lent you seventy thousand coins to travel west, how could you suddenly accuse me and cause me to die unjustly.'
  • 何负我之深也，言讫，遂跃而上 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of actions: 'how deeply have you wronged me, after finishing his words, he leaped up.'
  • 珪拽夜光，久之乃亡所见 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of actions: 'Zhang Tinggui pulled Yeguang, and after a long time, the vision disappeared.'
  • 师氏家僮咸见之，其后数日，夜光卒 - This phrase can be read as a sequence of events: 'all of Huidada's disciples saw it, a few days later, Yeguang died.'

  • 太守 - A term for a local magistrate or governor, common in the Han, Tang, and Song dynasties. It refers to an official who governed a county or region.

  • 士真生于王氏也 - This phrase implies that the scholar (士真) was born into the Wang (王) family. The term '士真' may be a name or title, possibly indicating a learned or virtuous person.
  • 唐荥阳郑生 - Zheng Sheng of Xingyang in the Tang dynasty. Xingyang (荥阳) is a historical county in Henan province, near Luoyang. The mention of the Tang dynasty situates the story in the 7th–10th century.
  • 骑射 - Refers to the martial arts of horseback riding and archery, commonly associated with military prowess and nobility in ancient China.
  • 勇悍趫捷 - Describes someone who is brave, fierce, agile, and quick, traits associated with warriors or heroes.
  • 巩雒之郊 - The suburbs of Gong and Luo. Luo likely refers to Luoyang (洛阳), the capital of the Eastern Han and later dynasties. Gong (巩) was a nearby city, and this phrase refers to the region around Luoyang.
  • 神庙 - A shrine or temple dedicated to a deity or spirit, common in Chinese religious and folk traditions.
  • 盗 - A thief or bandit, a common figure in classical Chinese tales and moral allegories.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 贞卢犹子 - Compound term where '贞' (zhēn) means 'loyal' or 'upright,' and '犹子' (yóu zǐ) refers to a younger brother or a close relative. Together, it likely refers to a loyal relative or a close family member.
  • 会昌中 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Wuzong of Tang (840–846 AD), specifically the year 845 AD, when the Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution occurred.
  • 沙汰僧徒 - Means 'to purge or expel monks,' referring to the persecution of Buddhist monks during the Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution.
  • 以荫补光王府参军 - '以荫' (yǐ yìn) means 'through hereditary privilege,' and '补' (bǔ) means 'to fill a vacancy.' '光王府参军' (Guang Wang Fu Canjun) refers to an official position in the Guang Wang's (Prince Guang's) household, specifically a military advisor.
  • 梦为僧时所奉师来慰 - '梦为僧时' (mèng wéi sēng shí) means 'in the dream of being a monk,' and '所奉师' (suǒ fèng shī) refers to the teacher or mentor revered during that time. '来慰' (lái wèi) means 'to come to comfort.'
  • 佛氏沦破 - '佛氏' (fó shì) refers to Buddhism, and '沦破' (lún pò) means 'to be destroyed' or 'to fall into ruin.'
  • 一官 - Refers to an official position or title.
  • 落顶上发 - '落顶' (luò dǐng) means 'to shave the head,' and '发' (fà) means 'hair.' This phrase refers to the act of shaving one's head, which is a symbolic act in Buddhism.
  • 期再落顶上发，方毕志愿 - '期再' (qī zài) means 'to hope to again,' and '方毕志愿' (fāng bì zhì yuàn) means 'to finally fulfill one's wishes.'
  • 宣宗自光邸践祚 - '宣宗' (Xuānzōng) refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, and '光邸' (Guāng Dǐ) refers to the Guang Wang's residence. '践祚' (jiàn zuò) means 'to ascend the throne.'
  • 录王府属吏 - '录' (lù) means 'to record' or 'to appoint,' and '王府属吏' (Wáng Fǔ Shǔ Lì) refers to officials under the Guang Wang's household.
  • 冥兆 - '冥' (míng) means 'mysterious' or 'spiritual,' and '兆' (zhào) means 'omen' or 'sign.' Together, it refers to a mysterious or spiritual sign.
  • 岂神之意，以是微而显乎 - '岂' (qǐ) means 'how can it be that,' '神之意' (shén zhī yì) means 'the will of the gods,' '以是' (yǐ shì) means 'by this,' and '微而显' (wēi ér xiǎn) means 'from subtle to clear.' This phrase questions whether the gods' will is manifested through subtle signs becoming clear.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The question '师病果愈乎吾之语岂妄耶' combines two clauses: 'Has the teacher's illness indeed recovered?' and 'Is my statement false?' The conjunction '乎' (乎) is used to connect the two clauses.
  • The sentence '可疾计修赤水神庙也' is elliptical and can be expanded to 'You should quickly plan to repair the Red River God Temple.'
  • The sentence '夫神所以赖于人者，以其福可延，戾可弭' explains the reason why gods rely on people, using the conjunction '以' (以) to introduce the cause.
  • The sentence '旱亢则雩之以泽，潦淫则萗之以霁' describes the rituals performed in response to natural disasters, using the conjunction '则' (则) to introduce the actions.
  • The sentence '天子诏天下郡国，虽一邑一里必建其祠' describes the emperor's decree, using the conjunction '虽' (虽) to emphasize the extent of the decree.
  • The sentence '若赤水神者，无以福人而为害于人' describes the nature of the Red River God, using the conjunction '而' (而) to contrast the deity's lack of benefits with its harmful effects.
  • The rhetorical question '焉可不去之' emphasizes the necessity of removing the deity, using the particle '焉' (焉) to express emphasis.
  • The sentence '已尽毁其庙矣' describes the action of destroying the temple, using the particle '已' (已) to indicate completion.
  • The phrase '且惊且惧' describes the emotional state of Yuan Sheng, using the conjunction '且' (且) to combine two emotions.
  • The sentence '遂谢之' describes the action of thanking, using the particle '遂' (遂) to indicate the sequence of events.
  • The sentence '道成气益丰，而袁生惧甚' describes the contrasting states of Daocheng and Yuan Sheng, using the conjunction '而' (而) to connect the two clauses.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence of actions in '夫人乃上堂，命引郑郎自西阶升' is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the formal tone.
  • The long sequence in '堂上悉以花罽荐地，左右施局脚床，七宝屏风，黄金屈膝，门垂碧箔，银钩珠络' must be read as a single description of the hall's furnishings and decorations.
  • The clause '长筵列馔，皆极丰洁' describes the banquet table and the food, requiring careful translation to convey the richness and cleanliness.
  • The simple command '乃命坐' must be translated in a formal manner.
  • The clause '夫人善清谈，叙置轻重，世难以比' describes the lady's skills and abilities, requiring careful translation to convey her exceptional qualities.
  • The description of the wine '食毕命酒，以银贮之，可三斗余，琥珀色，酌以镂杯，侍婢行酒，味极甘香' requires careful translation to convey its quality and value.
  • The sequence in '向暮，一婢前白，女郎已严妆讫' requires careful translation to maintain the formal tone and clarity.
  • The sequence in '乃命。引郑郎出就外间，浴以百味香汤，左右进衣冠履佩' requires careful translation to convey the sequence of events and the formality of the occasion.
  • The sequence in '美婢十人扶入，恣为调谑' requires careful translation to convey the interaction and the formality of the occasion.
  • The sequence in '自堂及门，步致花烛，乃延就帐' requires careful translation to convey the sequence of events and the formality of the occasion.
  • The description of the girl '女年十四五，姿色甚艳，目所未见' requires careful translation to convey her beauty and the formality of the occasion.
  • The description of the girl's attire '被服粲丽，冠绝当时' requires careful translation to convey her beauty and the formality of the occasion.
  • The simple command '郑遂欣然' must be translated in a formal manner.
  • The simple command '其后遂成礼' must be translated in a formal manner.
  • The sequence in '明日，夫人命女与就东堂' requires careful translation to maintain the formal tone and clarity.
  • The description of the hall '堂中置红罗绣帐，衾褥茵席，皆悉精绝' requires careful translation to convey the luxury and formality of the occasion.
  • The description of the girl's skills '女善弹箜篌，曲词新异' requires careful translation to convey her abilities and the formality of the occasion.
  • The question and answer sequence '郑问所迎婚前乘来马，今在何许，曰：“今已反矣。”' requires careful translation to convey the interaction and the formality of the occasion.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 林甫 - Refers to Li Linfu (李林甫), a powerful and corrupt chancellor of the Tang dynasty. The name is historically significant and must be rendered consistently.
  • 家僮 - Servants or attendants in the household – common in classical Chinese narratives of aristocratic or high-ranking figures.
  • 签 - Slips of paper, often used in divination or for naming purposes – in this context, they are slips bearing names.
  • 籍没 - Confiscation of property, a punishment for treason or corruption – a legal term from imperial China.
  • 十稔 - Ten years – '稔' is a term for a year's harvest, used here to mean a decade.

Proper Nouns

  • 长乐之地 - Changle (長樂) – likely a place associated with Li Linfu, possibly a residence or estate. If it is a proper place name, it should be transliterated as 'Changle.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 吾將邀之 - I will invite him. The phrase '邀之' is a common Classical Chinese construction for 'invite someone.'
  • 幸甚 - That would be wonderful. A polite expression indicating gratitude or approval.
  • 吾慕主人能詩，且好賓客 - I admire the host's ability to compose poetry and his fondness for guests. The phrase '慕' means admiration, and '好賓客' refers to enjoying the company of guests.
  • 步月至此 - Walking under the moonlight to get here. A poetic way of describing a leisurely journey.
  • 遂相談谑久之 - And we chatted and joked for a long time. The phrase '遂' indicates a continuation of an action, and '談谑' means chatting and joking.
  • 吾愛子也 - My beloved son. The phrase '愛子' emphasizes the affectionate relationship.
  • 少聰敏，好讀書 - He was young, intelligent, and fond of reading. The phrase '少' means young, '聰敏' means intelligent, and '好讀書' means fond of reading.
  • 因以青箱名焉 - I named him Qingxiang. The phrase '名焉' means to name something.
  • 欲使傳吾學也 - I wanted him to carry on my learning. The phrase '傳' means to pass on or transmit.
  • 不幸先吾逝 - Unfortunately, he passed away before me. The phrase '先吾逝' means to die before someone else.
  • 近従吾與仆射同過台城 - Recently, I accompanied the Governor and the Prefect through Taicheng. The phrase '従' means to accompany, and '過' means to pass through.
  • 因命為感舊 - I asked him to compose a poem about the past. The phrase '命為' means to order or ask someone to do something.
  • 援筆立成 - He picked up the brush and finished it immediately. The phrase '援筆' means to pick up a brush, and '立成' means to finish immediately.
  • 甚有可观 - It is very impressive. The phrase '有可观' means to be impressive or noteworthy.
  • 即讽之曰 - He then recited it, saying. The phrase '讽之' means to recite or recite aloud.

  • 繁华今寂寞 - Contrast between past prosperity ('繁华') and present desolation ('寂寞'), highlighting the theme of decline.
  • 朝市昔喧阗 - Refers to the bustling markets and streets of the past ('喧阗'), now silent or forgotten.
  • 夜月琉璃水 - Poetic image of the moonlight reflecting on the water, creating a shimmering, almost ethereal effect ('琉璃水' means 'glass-like water').
  • 春风卵色天 - Vivid description of the sky in spring, possibly referring to a pale or hazy color ('卵色' means 'eggshell color').
  • 伤时与怀古 - Compound expression meaning 'lamenting the present and yearning for the past,' capturing the dual emotions of the poem.
  • 垂泪国门前 - Literally 'shedding tears at the gate of the nation,' symbolizing sorrow or mourning for the country's fate.
  • 齐梁体 - Refers to a style of poetry from the Qi and Liang dynasties, characterized by its lack of strict adherence to rhyme and meter.
  • 律诗 - Refers to regulated verse, a form of poetry that emerged during the Tang dynasty, emphasizing strict rhyme and meter.
  • 青箱之诗 - Refers to poetry that follows the modern style ('青箱' is a metaphor for the modern era).
  • 竟陵之门 - Refers to the literary circle associated with the Jingle (竟陵) region, known for its distinctive poetic style.
  • 卢博 - Refers to a type of gambling or playful activity, symbolizing carefree enjoyment in the past.
  • 禅代 - Refers to the peaceful transfer of power, often used to describe the transition from one dynasty to another.
  • 佐命之臣 - Refers to loyal ministers who assist in the founding of a new dynasty.
  • 曩日之欢 - Refers to the joy of the past, contrasting with the current state of anxiety or sorrow.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 六代 - Refers to the Six Dynasties period (222–589 CE), a time of political fragmentation and cultural flourishing in Chinese history.
  • 昭明所集之《选》 - Refers to the *Wenxuan* (文选), a famous anthology of literature compiled by Xiao Tong (萧统), the Prince of Zhaoming, during the Liang dynasty.
  • 沈佺期、宋之问 - Two prominent poets of the Tang dynasty known for their mastery of regulated verse ('律诗').
  • 萧公禅代 - Refers to the peaceful transition of power from the Liang dynasty to the Sui dynasty, led by Emperor Wen of Sui (Yang Jian).
  • 竟陵 - A region in Hubei province, known for its literary and cultural significance during the Southern dynasties.

  • 廣陵有官舍 - Guangling has an official residence; refers to a government building in Guangling (present-day Yangzhou, Jiangsu).
  • 地步數百 - The area spans several hundred steps; describes the size of the residence.
  • 制置宏麗 - It is grandly constructed; indicates the impressive architecture of the residence.
  • 里中傳其中為鬼所宅 - In the neighborhood, it is said that a ghost inhabits it; refers to a local legend or superstition about the residence.
  • 故居之者一夕則暴死 - Anyone who stays there dies suddenly the same night; describes the supposed fatal outcome of staying in the residence.
  • 鎖閉累年矣 - It has been locked up for years; indicates that the residence has been abandoned and sealed off for a long time.
  • 妖不自作 - Evil does not arise on its own; a philosophical statement suggesting that evil actions have causes.
  • 我新居之，豈能為災耶 - If I move in, how could it bring disaster?; Cui's confident assertion that he will not be affected by any supposed evil.
  • 即白廉使而居焉 - He immediately informed the governor and moved in; describes Cui's decision to inform the local authority before moving in.
  • 惕然而寤 - He woke up with a start; describes Cui's sudden awakening due to fear or a supernatural experience.
  • 衣盡沾濕 - His clothes were completely soaked; indicates that he was drenched by something, possibly the rain or a supernatural occurrence.
  • 即起，見己之卧榻在庭中 - He got up and saw that his bed was in the courtyard; describes the strange phenomenon of his bed moving.
  • 卻寢，未食頃，其榻又遷於庭 - He went back to sleep, and before he had eaten, his bed moved again to the courtyard; repeats the strange phenomenon, emphasizing its persistence.
  • 如是者三 - This happened three times; indicates that the phenomenon occurred multiple times.
  • 我謂天下無鬼，今則果有矣 - I thought there were no ghosts in the world, but now I see that there are indeed; Cui's realization and admission of the existence of ghosts.
  • 即具簪笏，命酒，沃而祝曰 - He then put on his official cap and robes, poured wine, and made a toast, saying...; describes Cui's ritualistic response to the supernatural events.
  • 吾聞居此者多暴死 - I have heard that many who live here die suddenly; refers to the local legend about the residence.
  • 且人神殊道 - Moreover, humans and spirits follow different paths; a philosophical statement about the separation between humans and spirits.
  • 當自安其居，豈害生人耶 - They should stay in their own place and not harm living people; Cui's belief that the spirits should not interfere with human lives.
  • 雖苟以形見、以聲聞者 - Even if they appear in form or make sounds...; describes the possible manifestations of the spirits.
  • 是其負冤郁而將有訴者 - It is because they have grievances and are about to make a complaint; suggests that the spirits have reasons for their actions.
  • 或將求一飯以祭者 - Or they might be seeking a meal for a sacrifice; another possible reason for the spirits' actions.
  • 則見於人，而人自驚悸而死 - If they appear to people, people will be frightened and die; explains the supposed fatal outcomes.
  • 固非神靈害之也 - It is not that the spirits harm them; Cui's explanation that the deaths are not intentional acts of harm by the spirits.
  • 吾甚愚，且無畏惮 - I am very foolish and have no fear; Cui's self-deprecating statement, indicating his lack of fear.
  • 若真有所訴，直為我言 - If they truly have something to complain about, just tell me; Cui's invitation to the spirits to communicate with him.
  • 可以副汝托，雖湯火不避 - I can fulfill your trust, even if it means facing fire and water; Cui's promise to help the spirits, no matter the cost.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 誠不當以鬼干人 - Formal expression meaning 'it is not appropriate to disturb people with ghosts.' Here, '干' means 'to disturb' or 'to interfere.'
  • 直將以深誠奉告 - Means 'but will instead sincerely inform you.' '深誠' refers to deep sincerity.
  • 俱未笄而殁 - '笄' is a hairpin used in coming-of-age ceremonies for women. '未笄' means 'not yet of age.' '殁' means 'to die.'
  • 父母葬我於郡城之北久矣 - '郡城' refers to the city of the prefecture. '久矣' means 'for a long time.'
  • 工人伐我封内树且尽 - '封内' refers to the area around a grave. '伐' means 'to cut down.' '且尽' means 'almost all.'
  • 羁魂不宁 - '羁魂' means 'trapped soul' or 'restless spirit.' '不宁' means 'not at peace.'
  • 幸遇明君子 - '明君子' means 'enlightened gentleman' or 'wise and virtuous person.'
  • 傥君以仁心为我棺而葬于野 - '傥' is an archaic particle meaning 'if' or 'perhaps.' '仁心' means 'benevolent heart.' '棺' means 'coffin.' '葬于野' means 'bury in the wilderness.'
  • 真恩之大者矣 - '真恩' means 'true kindness.' '大者' means 'great.'
  • 某儿女子 - '某' is a humble self-reference. '儿女子' means 'girl' or 'young woman.'
  • 非有害于人也 - '非有害于人' means 'does not harm people.'
  • 吾前言固如是矣 - '固' means 'certainly' or 'indeed.' '如是' means 'as such.'
  • 如何不见我耶 - '如何' means 'why' or 'how.' '不见我' means 'cannot see me.'
  • 岂敢以幽晦之质而见君乎 - '岂敢' means 'how dare.' '幽晦之质' means 'dark and obscure nature.' '见君' means 'appear to you.'
  • 既诺我之请 - '诺' means 'promise' or 'agree.' '请' means 'request.'
  • 虽处冥昧中 - '冥昧' means 'darkness' or 'obscure state.'
  • 亦当感君子恩 - '亦当' means 'should also.' '感' means 'grateful.' '君子恩' means 'kindness of a gentleman.'
  • 岂可徒然而已 - '岂可' means 'how could.' '徒然' means 'in vain.' '而已' means 'just that.'
  • 已而涕泣鸣咽 - '已而' means 'then' or 'afterward.' '涕泣' means 'tears.' '鸣咽' means 'sobbing.'
  • 召工人 - '召' means 'call.' '工人' means 'workers.'
  • 于堂东北隅发之 - '于' means 'in.' '堂东北隅' means 'northeast corner of the hall.' '发之' means 'dig it up.'
  • 果得枯骸 - '果' means 'indeed' or 'actually.' '得' means 'find.' '枯骸' means 'dry bones.'
  • 葬于禅智寺隙地 - '葬于' means 'bury in.' '禅智寺' is the name of a temple. '隙地' means 'vacant land.'
  • 里人皆祭之 - '里人' means 'villagers.' '皆' means 'all.' '祭之' means 'worship it.'
  • 谓之三女坟 - '谓之' means 'call it.' '三女坟' means 'Trio of Girls' Grave.'
  • 自得其宅遂安 - '自得' means 'since obtaining.' '其宅' means 'its place.' '遂安' means 'became peaceful.'

  • This sequence provides historical context for the residence and the official's long-term stay.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 行十数里 - Literally 'walked ten or so li,' meaning 'walked a short distance.' In Classical Chinese, *里* (li) is a unit of distance, roughly 0.5 km.
  • 路曛黑不可辨 - A compound expression meaning 'the road was dark and indistinguishable.' *曛黑* is a classical term for dusk or darkness, and *不可辨* means 'could not be discerned.'
  • 但闻车马驰逐，人物喧语 - A compound expression meaning 'only heard the sound of carriages and horses galloping, and people speaking loudly.' *但闻* is 'only heard' or 'heard only.'
  • 亦闻其妻子兄弟呼者哭者 - 'Also heard his wife, children, and brothers calling and weeping.' *呼者哭者* is a classical structure used to describe people engaged in multiple actions simultaneously.
  • 且议丧具 - A phrase meaning 'are discussing funeral preparations.' *且* here functions as a conjunction, possibly meaning 'also' or 'now.'
  • 因自念 - 'Then he reflected to himself' or 'he thought to himself.'
  • 我今死矣 - 'I am now dead.' A direct statement of the speaker’s belief in his own death.
  • 当尽见亲友之殁者 - 'One should see all one’s deceased relatives and friends.' *当尽见* is a classical expression meaning 'should see all.'
  • 安知其不可哉 - 'How can I know it is not possible?' A rhetorical question expressing doubt or uncertainty.
  • 表弟 - A term meaning 'cousin by marriage' or 'nephew by marriage,' depending on the relationship.
  • 卒且数年 - 'Has been dead for several years.' *卒* is the verb 'to die,' and *且* here is used to indicate duration.
  • 与汶善 - 'Was close to Wen' or 'was on good terms with Wen.'
  • 果闻季伦应曰 - 'Indeed heard Jiulun respond, saying...' *果* is an adverb meaning 'indeed' or 'actually.'
  • 俱悲泣 - 'Both wept together.' *俱* is a conjunction meaning 'both' or 'together.'
  • 今弟之居，为何所也 - 'Now, where is your dwelling?' *何所也* is a classical way of asking 'where is it?'
  • 何为曛黑如是 - 'Why is it so dark?' *如是* is a classical way of saying 'like this' or 'so.'
  • 冥途幽晦 - 'The path of the dead is dark and obscure.' *冥途* is a classical term for the afterlife or the underworld.
  • 无日月之光故也 - 'Because there is no light of the sun or moon.' A classical explanation.
  • 恨不可尽 - 'Regrets cannot be fully expressed.' *恨* can mean 'regret' or 'sorrow.'
  • 今将去矣 - 'Now I must go.' A formal way of saying 'I must depart.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 丧具 - Refers to the equipment or items needed for a funeral, including coffins, incense, and offerings. In Chinese funerary culture, proper preparation of *丧具* is essential for the deceased to reach the afterlife.
  • 冥途 - A classical term for the afterlife or the underworld. In Daoist and Buddhist cosmology, the *冥途* is a dark and mysterious realm where the dead travel.
  • 幽晦 - A term used to describe the darkness and obscurity of the afterlife, often associated with the *冥途* or the *黄泉* (Yellow Springs), the underworld in Chinese mythology.
  • 日月之光 - Refers to the sun and moon, symbols of light and clarity in Chinese cosmology. The absence of their light in the *冥途* reflects the spiritual and physical darkness of the afterlife.
  • 表弟 - A familial term, indicating a cousin by marriage or a nephew by marriage. This suggests a close but not immediate familial relationship.
  • 武季伦 - A proper name, likely a historical or fictional character. No known historical figure of this name is documented, so it must be treated as a fictional or literary character.
  • 汶 - A proper name, likely the name of the narrator or protagonist. No known historical figure of this name is documented, so it must be treated as a fictional or literary character.

  • 见諲具巾笏，率庭掾分立于庭 - 'saw Yin with his headgear and笏 (a tablet used in court), leading the court officials to stand in rows in the courtyard.'
  • 咸挽而拱 - 'all of them bowed and clasped their hands.'
  • 云中有一人，紫衣金鱼，执一幅书，宣道帝命 - Describes a person in a purple robe with golden fish decorations, holding a scroll, and announcing the emperor's command.
  • 使驾云而上，顷之遂没 - 'he was lifted up by the cloud and soon disappeared.'
  • 諲命崔君出坐 - 'Yin ordered Cui to come out and sit.'
  • 启天符视之，且叹且泣 - 'opened the heavenly symbol to look at it, and both sighed and cried.'
  • 子识元三乎 - 'do you know Yuan San?'
  • 乃布衣之旧耳 - 'he is just an old acquaintance from my days as a commoner.'
  • 血属无类 - 'no blood relations are alike.'
  • 吁，可悲夫 - Expression of sorrow, meaning 'Alas, how sad!'
  • 某虽与元三为友，至是亦无能拯之，徒积悲叹 - 'Although I am friends with Yuan San, by now I can no longer save him, only accumulating grief and sighs.'
  • 词已又泣 - 'after finishing speaking, he wept again.'
  • 既而命一吏送崔君归 - 'then he ordered a clerk to send Cui back.'
  • 再拜而出，与使者俱行，入郡城廨中 - 'bowed twice and left, accompanied by the messenger, entering the government office in the county town.'
  • 已身卧于榻，妻孥辈哭而环之 - 'he was already lying on a bed, with his wife and children crying around him.'
  • 使者引崔俯于榻，魂与身翕然而合，遂寤 - 'the messenger led Cui to bow before the bed, and his spirit and body merged, and he woke up.'
  • 其家云：“卒三日矣。” - 'his family said, 'He has been dead for three days.''
  • 本郡以白廉使 - 'the county reported this to the censor.'
  • 崔即治装，尽室往蜀 - 'Cui immediately packed his belongings, and with his entire family went to Shu.'
  • 具告于宁 - 'he fully informed Ning.'
  • 宁遂署摄副使，月给俸钱二十万 - 'Ning then appointed Cui as acting deputy governor, giving him a monthly salary of 200,000 coins.'
  • 时元载方执国政，宁与载善，书遣甚多 - 'at that time, Yuan Zai was in control of the state affairs, and Ning had a good relationship with him, sending many letters.'
  • 闻崔之言，惧其连坐，因命亲吏赍五百金，赂载左右，尽购得其收百余幅，皆焚之 - 'upon hearing Cui's words, he feared being implicated and ordered his close officials to carry 500 gold pieces, bribing Yuan Zai's attendants, buying and burning over a hundred scrolls.'
  • 又二年，崔亦终矣 - 'two years later, Cui also died.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 冥路 - Refers to the path of the dead, often used in literature to describe the afterlife.

  • 寇天师谦之 - Refers to Master Kou Qianzhi, a famous Taoist priest of the Northern Wei dynasty, known for his spiritual practices and writings.

  • 木子当天下 - A prediction about the Tang dynasty, where 'wood' (木) and 'son' (子) together form 'Li,' the surname of the Tang emperors.
  • 止戈龙 - A prediction about Empress Wu Zetian, where '止戈' (stop the sword) and '龙' (dragon) combine to form '武' (Wu), her surname.
  • 李代代不可移宗 - A prediction about the continuity of the Tang dynasty, emphasizing the stability of the Li lineage.
  • 中鼎显真容 - A prediction about Emperor Zhenzong, where '中鼎' (middle of the tripod) and '显真容' (reveal the true form) combine to form '真' (Zhen), his posthumous title.
  • 基千万岁 - A prediction about the longevity of the Tang dynasty, where '基' (foundation) and '千万岁' (a thousand years) indicate the dynasty's enduring power.
  • 玄宗 - Refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, a ruler of the Tang dynasty.
  • 中宗 - Refers to Emperor Zhongzong of Tang, a ruler of the Tang dynasty.
  • 国史 - Refers to the official national history, a record of the country's past events and achievements.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 冥路杳杳人不知，不用苦说使人悲 - This couplet can be read as a single unit, emphasizing the unknowability of the afterlife and the futility of unnecessary sorrow.
  • 喜得逢君传家信，后会茫茫何处期 - This couplet can be read as a single unit, emphasizing the joy of sharing important news and the uncertainty of future reunions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 水溢流，注滿四野 - Describes a flood spreading widely; 'water overflows, filling the four fields.'
  • 蛟螭之血，遍若玄黄 - Metaphor for chaotic and mixed nature of the flood; 'the blood of the dragon and the serpent spread like black and yellow.'
  • 有凿成文字一十九言，字勢甚古 - Refers to an ancient inscription with nineteen characters; 'there are carved words, nineteen in number, with a very ancient style.'
  • 郡中士庶，無能知者 - Indicates local scholars and commoners could not understand the inscription; 'the scholars and commoners of the county could not understand it.'
  • 自是居人無復患矣 - Indicates the resolution of a problem; 'since then, the residents no longer suffered.'
  • 遷者亦歸，結屋架廬，接比其地 - Describes the reestablishment of the community; 'those who had moved also returned, building houses and huts, connecting their land.'
  • 郡守因之名其地為“石銘里” - Explains the naming of the place; 'the prefecture governor named the place 'Shi Ming Li' because of it.'
  • 蓋因字為銘，且識其異也 - Explains the rationale behind the name; 'it was named because of the characters as a memorial, and to recognize its uniqueness.'
  • 後有客於泉者，能傳其字 - Introduces a guest who transmitted the characters; 'later, a guest from the spring was able to transmit the characters.'
  • 持至東洛 - Specifies the destination; 'he brought it to Dong Luo.'
  • 故吏部侍郎韓愈自尚書郎為河南令，見而識之 - Provides context about the person who deciphered the text; 'the former Ministry of Personnel Deputy Secretary Han Yu, who was a Prefect of Henan from the position of Secretary of the Ministry of Rites, saw and recognized it.'
  • 其文曰：“詔示黑水鯉魚，天公卑殺牛人，壬癸神書急急。” - Provides the content of the inscription; 'the text reads: 'Edict to the black water carp, Heaven's deity humbles the ox-slaughtering man, the divine book of Ren and Gui is urgent.'
  • 然則詳究其義，似上帝責蛟螭之詞令，戮其害也 - Interprets the text's purpose; 'thus, examining its meaning in detail, it seems to be a command from the Heavenly Deity to punish the dragon and serpent for their harm.'
  • 其字則蝌蚪篆書，故泉人無有識者矣 - Explains why the locals could not read the text; 'the characters are in the form of seal script, so the people of the spring did not recognize them.'

  • 天公 - Refers to the Heavenly Deity, a central figure in Chinese mythology.
  • 壬癸 - Refers to the days of the week in the Chinese calendar, specifically the last two days of the week.
  • 神書 - Refers to a divine or sacred book, often associated with religious or mystical texts.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '尝一夕，与其友数辈会食于其室' introduces the setting and the characters, but the structure is somewhat elliptical.
  • The sentence '俄有一妪，容服洁白，长二尺许，出室之北隅，缓步而来' describes the appearance and actions of the old woman, but the description is compact and requires careful unpacking.
  • The sentence '其妪渐迫其榻，且语曰：“君有会，不能不命耶何待吾之薄欤！”' combines action and dialogue, requiring clear separation of the two elements.
  • The sentence '吕生叱之，遂退去，至北隅乃亡所见' describes the reaction and the disappearance of the old woman, but the sequence of events is compressed.
  • The sentence '明日，生独寤于室，又见其妪在北隅下，将前且退，惶然若有所惧' continues the narrative, describing the second encounter with the old woman, but the emotional state of the protagonist is emphasized.
  • The sentence '生又叱之，遂没' describes the third encounter, with a similar structure to the previous one.
  • The sentence '明日，生默念曰：“是必怪也。今夕将至，若不除之，必为吾患不朝夕矣。”' introduces the protagonist's internal monologue and his decision to take action.
  • The sentence '即命一剑置其榻下' describes the preparation for the encounter, but the action is implied rather than explicitly stated.
  • The sentence '是夕，果自北隅徐步而来，颜色不惧，至榻前' describes the third encounter in detail, with a focus on the old woman's demeanor.
  • The sentence '生以剑挥之' describes the protagonist's action, but the sequence of events is compressed.
  • The sentence '其妪忽上榻，以臂揕生胸，余又跃于左右，举袂而舞' describes the old woman's actions in detail, but the sequence of events is complex.
  • The sentence '久之，又有一妪忽上榻，复以臂揕生' continues the description of the old woman's actions, with a focus on the intensity of the encounter.
  • The sentence '生遽觉一身尽凛然，若霜被于体' describes the protagonist's physical and emotional reaction, but the imagery is vivid and requires careful translation.
  • The sentence '生又以剑乱挥，俄有数妪亦随而舞焉' describes the final encounter, with a focus on the supernatural elements.

  • 色黑而大，有光，視之潔徹，若輕冰焉 - Black in color, large in size, with a glow; when looked at, it is clean and transparent, like light ice.
  • 生持以示於人 - Yan Sheng showed it to people.
  • 常置于箱中 - He often placed it in a box.
  • 其后生游长安 - Later, Yan Sheng traveled to Chang’an.
  • 乃于春明门逢一胡人 - At the Spring Ming Gate, he met a Hu person.
  • 叩焉而言 - He approached and said.
  • 衣橐中有奇宝 - I have a strange treasure in my bag.
  • 愿有得一见 - I wish to see it once.
  • 此宝安所用而君厚其价如是哉 - What use is this treasure to you that you value it so highly?
  • 我，西国人 - I am a person from the Western lands.
  • 此乃吾国之至宝，国人谓之‘清水珠’ - This is the most precious treasure of my country, which the people call 'Clear Water Pearl.'
  • 若置于浊水，泠然洞彻矣 - If placed in murky water, it will become cold and clear.
  • 自亡此宝且三岁 - Since losing this treasure, it has been nearly three years.
  • 吾国之井泉尽浊，国人俱病 - All the wells and springs in my country have become murky, and the people have all fallen ill.
  • 故此越海逾山，来中夏以求之 - Therefore, I have crossed over mountains and seas to come to the Central Kingdom to seek it.
  • 今果得于子矣 - Now, I have indeed obtained it from you.
  • 胡人即命注浊水于缶，以珠投之 - The Hu person immediately ordered murky water to be poured into a jar and cast the pearl into it.
  • 俄而其水澹然清莹，纤毫可辩 - Soon the water became clear and bright, and even the smallest details could be seen.
  • 生于是以珠与胡，获其价而去 - Yan Sheng then gave the pearl to the Hu person, received the price, and left.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 嚴生 - A name, possibly a fictional or historical figure. No known historical figure by this name is documented in classical sources, so it is likely a character in a story or anecdote.
  • 漢南 - A geographical region, likely in the Han dynasty, referring to the area south of the Han River in modern-day Hubei province.
  • 彈丸 - A small projectile, often used in ancient games or for shooting. In this context, it is used as a metaphor or simile for the size of the object.
  • 彈珠 - A name given by the character, combining *彈* (projectile) and *珠* (pearl), suggesting a small, shiny object.
  • 春明门 - A specific gate in Chang’an, the capital of the Han and Tang dynasties. It was a symbol of entry into the imperial city.
  • 胡人 - Refers to people from the Western Regions, including Central Asia, often associated with trade, culture, and foreign influence in Chinese history.
  • 西国 - A term for the Western Regions or foreign lands, used to distinguish non-Han Chinese people or cultures.
  • 清水珠 - A specific name for the object, suggesting its function of purifying water. This is likely a mythical or legendary object.
  • 中夏 - A term for China or the Central Kingdom, used in contrast to foreign lands.
  • 缶 - An ancient Chinese clay vessel used for holding liquids, often in ritual or domestic contexts.

Proper Nouns

  • 嚴生 - A character name; should be consistently translated as *Yan Sheng*.
  • 漢南 - A geographical term; should be translated as *Han Nan* or *south of the Han River*.
  • 彈珠 - A name given to the object; should be translated as *dan zhu* or *ball pearl*.
  • 春明门 - A specific gate in Chang’an; should be translated as *Spring Ming Gate*.
  • 胡人 - A term for people from the Western Regions; should be translated as *Hu person* or *Westerner*.
  • 西国 - A term for the Western Regions or foreign lands; should be translated as *Western land* or *Western country*.
  • 清水珠 - A specific name for the object; should be translated as *Clear Water Pearl*.
  • 中夏 - A term for China or the Central Kingdom; should be translated as *Central Kingdom* or *China*.
  • 缶 - A type of ancient vessel; should be translated as *jar*.
